trigger: rumor of second major company hitting scaling wall
quote: scaling laws" are not physical laws. They are merely empirical generalizations that held for a certain period time, when there was enough fresh data and compute
claim: AI scaling laws are not physical laws but empirical generalizations that held only during a period of sufficient fresh data and compute

trigger: rumor of second major company hitting scaling wall
quote: there has never been any principled argument that they would solve hallucinations or reasoning, or edge cases in open-ended worlds
claim: There has never been a principled argument that scaling would solve hallucinations, reasoning failures, or edge cases in open-ended domains

trigger: rumor of second major company hitting scaling wall
quote: I strongly suspect that, contra Altman, we have in fact reached a point of diminishing returns for pure scaling
claim: Pure scaling of LLMs has reached a point of diminishing returns

trigger: rumor of second major company hitting scaling wall
quote: Just adding data and compute and training longer worked miracles for a while, but those days may well be over
claim: The era when simply adding data, compute, and training time yielded major improvements in LLMs is likely over

trigger: rumor of second major company hitting scaling wall
quote: If the rumor is verified, there could be the AI equivalent of a bank run.
claim: If multiple major AI companies confirm hitting scaling walls, it could trigger a massive loss of investor confidence resembling a bank run

trigger: study claiming ChatGPT poetry indistinguishable from Shakespeare
quote: hopes he has taught you by now to never trust the hype
claim: AI hype around capabilities like poetry generation should not be trusted at face value

quote: the so-called [LLM] scaling laws aren't universal laws like gravity but rather mere observations that might not hold forever
claim: LLM scaling laws are not universal laws but empirical observations that may not hold forever

quote: we may already [be] approaching a point of diminishing returns
claim: LLM training may already be at a point of diminishing returns

quote: Hundreds of billions of dollars were invested on the assumption that I was wrong, largely to the exclusion of investment in other approaches. Almost a half decade lost, when we could have been seriously investing in other approaches
claim: Hundreds of billions of dollars invested in pure LLM scaling came at the expense of investment in alternative AI approaches, wasting nearly half a decade

quote: symbol-manipulation, as discussed in his 2001 book The Algebraic Mind and again in the second half of "Deep learning is hitting a wall" will be part of the solution
claim: Symbol-manipulation will be part of the solution for achieving robust, trustworthy AI

trigger: Satya Nadella introducing inference-time compute scaling at Microsoft Ignite
quote: the new scaling "laws" are very much like the first – they are empirical generalizations, not physical laws. Like the first generalization, the new ones will eventually run out
claim: Inference-time compute scaling laws are empirical generalizations that will eventually run out, just like training-time scaling laws

trigger: Satya Nadella introducing inference-time compute scaling at Microsoft Ignite
quote: the alleged "scaling" law (inspired by OpenAI's o1) that Nadella emphasized— the more time you spend on a problem, the more accurate you get —is (a) expensive, because it by definition burns up more GPU time and (b) unreliable
claim: Inference-time compute scaling (as in o1) is both expensive due to GPU usage and unreliable in its improvements

trigger: Satya Nadella introducing inference-time compute scaling at Microsoft Ignite
quote: My guess is o1's test time scaling works best in closed domains where you can create a lot of reliable synthetic data (such as math problems), and not so well in open-ended domains
claim: Test-time compute scaling (as in o1) works best in closed domains amenable to synthetic data generation and poorly in open-ended domains

trigger: Satya Nadella introducing inference-time compute scaling at Microsoft Ignite
quote: GPT-4 was better than GPT-3 in almost every way; o1 is a far less uniform improvement
claim: o1 represents a far less uniform improvement over GPT-4 than GPT-4 was over GPT-3

trigger: Satya Nadella introducing inference-time compute scaling at Microsoft Ignite
quote: "Scaling" used to mean "I can predict pretty much exactly how well a given LLM will do, by knowing the size of its database and the amount of compute being used to train it, and those increases will be exponential". Now it just means "I think it will get better, not sure how much, if I can keep tweaking some parameter"
claim: The meaning of 'scaling' in AI has been devalued from precise predictive power to vague hope of improvement through parameter tweaking

trigger: Satya Nadella introducing inference-time compute scaling at Microsoft Ignite
quote: Trying to rescue LLMs by making a system that is inherently blind to factuality run longer — rather than better—is like smearing water around wet plates
claim: LLMs are inherently blind to factuality, and running them longer (inference-time scaling) does not fix this fundamental limitation

trigger: Satya Nadella introducing inference-time compute scaling at Microsoft Ignite
quote: We can and must do better. Scaling can only take us so far; the time has come for fresh invention.
claim: Scaling alone cannot achieve AI's goals; fundamentally new inventions and approaches are needed

quote: Every big tech company is desperate for training data, and we can expect more companies following the same sly opt-out playbook, hiding what is going in obscure settings and Terms of Service nobody reads
claim: Big tech companies are desperate for AI training data and will increasingly use sly opt-out tactics hidden in obscure settings to scrape user data

quote: none of us stand any chance against big tech unless we stand together
claim: Individuals cannot effectively resist big tech's data practices unless they act collectively

quote: hopes that the Trump administration will take AI regulation seriously
claim: The Trump administration should take AI regulation seriously

trigger: Microsoft response to training data scraping allegations
quote: Absent a clear statement in the Terms of Service, I personally remain uncertain
claim: Microsoft's denial that it uses Office 365 customer data for LLM training is not adequately supported by clear documentation and remains uncertain

quote: a lot of of theories about how ChatGPT would be used in practice have fizzled out
claim: Many predicted use cases for ChatGPT have failed to materialize in practice

quote: Google's search share hasn't diminished all that much
claim: ChatGPT has not significantly diminished Google's search market share despite predictions it would

quote: a lot of Fortune 500 companies are somewhat underwhelmed by the real world results they are getting
claim: Many Fortune 500 companies are underwhelmed by real-world results from generative AI

quote: There has been a ton of experimentation, but relatively few well-documented success stories
claim: Despite widespread experimentation with generative AI, there are relatively few well-documented enterprise success stories

quote: These systems have no conception of truth. Sometimes they land on it and sometimes they don't, but they're all fundamentally bullshitting
claim: LLMs have no conception of truth and are fundamentally generating plausible-sounding text without regard for factuality

quote: even today hallucinations (probably better described as confabulations) still haven't gone away. And not for lack of trying
claim: LLM hallucinations/confabulations persist despite extensive industry efforts to eliminate them

quote: The industry has tried practically everything, scaling, more scaling, and RAG. Apple even tried (to little avail) literally putting "do not hallucinate" in its system prompt. None of this has eliminated the problem.
claim: Neither scaling, RAG, nor system prompt instructions have eliminated the LLM hallucination problem

quote: Two years on, on the most important question of all – factuality and reliability — we are still pretty much where we were when ChatGPT first came out
claim: On factuality and reliability, LLMs have made little meaningful progress in the two years since ChatGPT's launch

quote: RAG, scaling, and system problems haven't eradicated the inherent tendency of LLMs to hallucinate
claim: The tendency of LLMs to hallucinate is inherent to the technology and has not been eradicated by RAG, scaling, or system prompts

quote: Commercial progress has been halting precisely because the tech simply isn't reliable
claim: The lack of commercial progress in generative AI is caused by the technology's fundamental unreliability

quote: hundreds of billions more have been invested on further speculation that scaling would somehow magically cure problems that actually appear to be inherent with the technology
claim: Problems like hallucination appear to be inherent to LLM technology rather than solvable through scaling alone

quote: neurosymbolic AI will be part of the deeper breakthroughs we need
claim: Neurosymbolic AI will be part of the deeper breakthroughs needed to advance AI beyond current LLM limitations

quote: one of the biggest applications of AI seems to be ripping off customers
claim: One of the biggest current applications of AI is being used to deny legitimate customer claims and rip off consumers

quote: AI doesn't have to lead to dystopia. But, left unregulated, it probably will.
claim: Without regulation, AI will probably lead to dystopian outcomes

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: If there is even a 5% chance I am wrong about diminishing returns, we still ought to be moving immediately to prepare
claim: Even with a low probability that LLM scaling will continue to improve rapidly, society should immediately prepare for powerful AI through regulation

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: we still really cannot guarantee that any given system will be honest, harmless, or helpful, rather than sycophantic, dishonest, toxic or biased
claim: Current AI systems cannot be guaranteed to be honest, harmless, or helpful rather than sycophantic, dishonest, toxic, or biased

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: AI needs a dedicated regulator
claim: AI needs a dedicated government regulatory body

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: the whole ballgame is whether the $200+B investment in Generative AI is the right path to AI or not
claim: The central question in AI is whether the $200+ billion investment in generative AI is the right path to AI

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: thinking that if an AI is stupid (or overrated) it can't be dangerous. As I have written multiple times before this is not so
claim: AI systems can be both limited/overrated and simultaneously dangerous; stupidity and danger are not mutually exclusive

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: a stupid AI that is blind to facts can be misused to create propaganda, or create deepfakes
claim: Even unreliable AI systems can be misused to create propaganda and deepfakes

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: we will get to AGI, but the field has been pursuing a hypothesis that was basically (just as I anticipated) disproven, so we will need new ideas
claim: AGI will eventually be achieved but will require new ideas beyond current LLM approaches, which have been shown to have diminishing returns

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: LLM's in particular are wildly overrated
claim: LLMs are wildly overrated relative to what has been promised of them

trigger: Casey Newton essay on AI skepticism
quote: LLMs are already dangerous, despite their limits. Covert racism? Deep fakes? Propaganda? Discrimination in employment, insurance and housing?
claim: LLMs are already dangerous due to enabling covert racism, deepfakes, propaganda, and discrimination in employment, insurance, and housing

quote: As far as these experiments go, DALL-E3 in November 2024 is slightly improved over DALL-E2 in October 2022, but only slightly
claim: DALL-E 3 in November 2024 shows only slight improvement over DALL-E 2 from October 2022 on part-specification tasks

quote: current technology has proven quite poor at such sanity checks. New technology may be needed
claim: Current AI image generation technology cannot reliably check whether its output matches the prompt; new technology may be needed

quote: the relation between language and images remains superficial and unpredictable in current approaches. Any one trial might be correct, but there is never much of a guarantee
claim: Current AI approaches to relating language and images remain superficial and unpredictable, with no guarantee of correctness

quote: Relating language and the visual world robustly may require altogether new approaches to learning language. Until then the foundations of AI remain unsteady
claim: Robustly relating language and the visual world may require entirely new approaches; the foundations of current AI remain unsteady

quote: More data won't solve [Sora's] problem [with physics]
claim: More training data will not solve Sora's problems with physics simulation

quote: like other generative AI systems, there is no way to encode (and guarantee) constraints like "be truthful" or "obey the laws of physics" or "don't just invent (or eliminate) objects"
claim: There is no way to encode and guarantee physical law constraints in generative AI video systems like Sora

quote: The John Locke hypothesis that you can learn physics purely from sense data is failing, over, and over, and over again
claim: The approach of learning physics purely from sensory data (video) is failing repeatedly in AI systems

trigger: Casey Newton follow-up on AI skepticism
quote: the question, which Newton sidesteps, is whether improvements continue at the same pace
claim: The key question about AI progress is whether improvements continue at the same pace as 2020-2022, and evidence suggests they do not

trigger: Casey Newton follow-up on AI skepticism
quote: 18 months of results that look more incremental than exponential
claim: AI model improvements over the 18 months preceding December 2024 have been incremental rather than exponential

trigger: Casey Newton follow-up on AI skepticism
quote: the leap from 34.3 to 35.1 on LiveBench is hardly a dramatic improvement
claim: Gemini 2 shows only modest improvement over Gemini 1.5, consistent with diminishing returns in LLM performance

trigger: Casey Newton follow-up on AI skepticism
quote: In 1998 I warned that neural networks would have trouble dealing with abstract reasoning and outlier cases, a finding that has held steady for the last 26 years
claim: Neural networks have persistent trouble with abstract reasoning and outlier cases, a limitation that has held for over 26 years

trigger: Casey Newton follow-up on AI skepticism
quote: Cruise put 9 billion into driverless cars, presumably based on optimism, and just yesterday shut their entire robotaxi operation down
claim: AI optimism led to massive failed investments like Cruise's $9 billion in driverless cars before shutting down entirely

trigger: Casey Newton follow-up on AI skepticism
quote: OpenAI has yet to make a profit, and doesn't anticipate making one before 2029
claim: OpenAI has not made a profit and does not expect to be profitable before 2029

trigger: Casey Newton follow-up on AI skepticism
quote: you can't improve the engineering if you can't identify the problems and weak points, such that you can address them
claim: AI skepticism that identifies specific problems and weak points is essential for engineering progress

trigger: Apollo Research paper on LLM scheming
quote: nobody should ever take LLMs at their word. They can easily tell you one thing and (especially if hooked up as agents) do another — possibly quite contrary to what they have alleged they are doing
claim: LLMs should never be taken at their word because they can claim to be doing one thing while actually doing something entirely different, especially when deployed as agents

trigger: Apollo Research paper on LLM scheming
quote: They have lots of examples in the database — and little capacity to compute the internal consistency of their own actions
claim: LLMs lack the capacity to compute the internal consistency of their own actions

trigger: Apollo Research paper on LLM scheming
quote: they do not actually calculate the consequences of their actions at all. They can't reliably sanity check themselves for hallucinations, and they can't reliably sanity check for whether what they are doing is harmful
claim: LLMs cannot calculate the consequences of their actions and cannot reliably self-check for hallucinations or harmful behavior

trigger: Apollo Research paper on LLM scheming
quote: They more power we give them (but hooking up them to agents, people, etc), the more risk we are incurring
claim: Giving LLMs more power through agent frameworks and external connections increases risk

trigger: Apollo Research paper on LLM scheming
quote: Even if machines don't ever rise up a la the Terminator, a bad actor could still leverage LLMs to cause massive mayhem. Possibly soon.
claim: Bad actors could leverage LLMs to cause massive harm in the near term, even without AGI or machine uprising

trigger: Apollo Research paper on LLM scheming
quote: Expect a lot of GenAI-driven cybercrime in the coming years
claim: GenAI-driven cybercrime will increase significantly in the coming years

trigger: Apollo Research paper on LLM scheming
quote: The longer we delay in regulation, the more risk we are incurring
claim: Delays in AI regulation increase the cumulative risk from AI systems

quote: OpenAI's newly-released video generation system Sora seems, like many prior systems, to have been extensively trained on copyrighted materials
claim: OpenAI's Sora video generation system appears to have been extensively trained on copyrighted materials

quote: it too seems prone at least some of the time to disgorging equally uncreative, near plagiaristic outputs
claim: Sora, like prior generative AI systems, sometimes produces near-plagiaristic outputs derived from copyrighted training data

quote: Society needs to do more to protect people like Suchir Balaji
claim: Society needs stronger whistleblower protections for people who challenge AI companies on issues like copyright

quote: Until lots of people get to try o3 on many different kinds of tasks, we should not assume that it is reliable
claim: o3 should not be assumed reliable until it has been broadly tested by the public across many different task types

quote: At best, I would wager, it will be reliable for some kinds of problems, and not others
claim: o3 will likely be reliable for some kinds of problems but not others, rather than being a general breakthrough

quote: I saw zero evidence that o3 could work reliably in open-ended domains
claim: The o3 demo provided no evidence that it can work reliably in open-ended domains outside math, coding, and IQ-like puzzles

quote: It is also insanely expensive. By which I mean that at least for now far far more expensive than average humans
claim: o3 is currently far more expensive than average humans, with individual calls estimated to cost around $1000

quote: Spending a quarter trillion dollars in order to have something 1000 times more expensive than average humans is maybe not the way to go
claim: The economics of spending a quarter trillion dollars on AI that is 1000x more expensive than human labor are questionable

quote: the impact of all this on the environment could be immense
claim: The environmental impact of inference-time scaling approaches like o3 could be immense

quote: If it were truly robust across all or even most problems, they would have called it GPT-5. The fact that they didn't is a strong hint that its scope is importantly limited.
claim: OpenAI not calling o3 'GPT-5' is strong evidence that its scope is importantly limited and it is not a general improvement

quote: Passing ARC-AGI does not equate to achieving AGI
claim: Passing the ARC-AGI benchmark does not equate to achieving AGI (Marcus endorsing Chollet's position)

quote: neural networks tend to be better at generalizing "within distribution" than "outside distribution". An important new paper just out by Qin, Saphra, and Alvarez-Mellis makes this point yet again
claim: Neural networks remain better at generalizing within-distribution than outside-distribution, a fundamental limitation that persists

quote: Nothing in the o3 presentation yesterday suggested to me that this decades-old core problem has been remotely solved
claim: o3 does not appear to have solved the decades-old problem of neural network out-of-distribution generalization

quote: we shouldn't take demos seriously, until purported products are actually released and subjected to outside scrutiny
claim: AI demos should not be taken seriously until the products are released and subjected to independent external scrutiny

quote: The video should have been much clearer about what was actually tested and what was actually trained. To the average listener it may have sounded like the AI took the test cold, with a few sample items, like a human would, but that's not actually what happened
claim: OpenAI's o3 demo was misleading because it did not make clear that the model was pretrained on hundreds of public ARC examples, unlike how a human would take the test

quote: What was actually done - pretraining on what I believe was hundreds of public examples - is NOT comparable to what humans require
claim: o3's pretraining on hundreds of ARC examples makes its performance not comparable to human performance on the same test

quote: the best thing would have been to present data for the "base model" without the pretraining. This is what many people thought they saw, and that # is important scientifically. Unfortunately the key test was not done
claim: The scientifically important test of o3's base model performance on ARC without task-specific pretraining was not conducted

quote: What we saw is not AGI. Both Chollet and OpenAI's Anup made this clear, but only after the live video.
claim: o3 is not AGI, and this was only clarified after the live demo, not during it

quote: People in the media probably shouldn't even joke about o3 being AGI. The media should be asking hard questions, not fanning hype
claim: Media should not promote the narrative that o3 is AGI or close to it, and should instead ask hard questions about its limitations

quote: Both of us agree that the achievement of 8 out of 10 of these tasks would be strong evidence for the G (General) in Artificial General Intelligence having been achieved
claim: Achieving 8 out of 10 specific cognitive tasks (movie comprehension, novel reading, biography writing, video games, legal briefs, coding, books, screenplays, scientific discovery, math proof formalization) would constitute strong evidence for AGI

quote: If there exist AI systems that can perform 8 of the 10 tasks below by the end of 2027, as determined by our panel of judges, Gary will donate $2,000 to a charity of Miles' choice; if AI can do fewer than 8, Miles will donate $20,000 to a charity of Gary's choice
claim: Marcus bets at 10:1 odds that AI will not be able to perform 8 of 10 specified AGI-level tasks by end of 2027

quote: We are still basically surrounded by GPT-4 level models with incremental changes, and nothing that OpenAI themselves thinks is worthy of the GPT-5 label
claim: As of early 2025, AI models remain at roughly GPT-4 level with only incremental changes, and no model qualifies as GPT-5

quote: There are many such models now; there is a price war. There is very little moat
claim: The AI model market has many similar models, is experiencing a price war, and companies have very little competitive moat

quote: There is still no robust solution to hallucinations
claim: There is still no robust solution to LLM hallucinations as of early 2025

quote: Corporate adoption is far more limited than most people expected, and total profits across all companies (except of course hardware companies like NVidia, which profits from chips rather than models) have been modest at best
claim: Corporate AI adoption is far more limited than expected and total profits for AI model companies have been modest at best

quote: My predictions, going back to 2022 that pure scaling of LLMs would eventually start to run out appear to have been confirmed in November and December
claim: The prediction that pure LLM scaling would run out was confirmed by industry insider comments in November-December 2024

quote: We will not see artificial general intelligence this year, despite claims by Elon Musk to the contrary
claim: AGI will not be achieved in 2025, despite Elon Musk's prediction

quote: No single system will solve more than 4 of the AI 2027 Marcus-Brundage tasks by the end of 2025
claim: No single AI system will solve more than 4 of the 10 Marcus-Brundage AGI tasks by end of 2025

quote: Profits from AI models will continue to be modest or nonexistent
claim: Profits from AI models will remain modest or nonexistent in 2025, though hardware companies like Nvidia will continue to profit

quote: The US will continue to have very little regulation protecting its consumers from the risks of generative AI
claim: The US will continue to have very little consumer-protective AI regulation in 2025

quote: The lack of reliability will continue to haunt generative AI
claim: Generative AI will continue to suffer from reliability problems throughout 2025

quote: Hallucinations (which should really be called confabulations) will continue to haunt generative AI
claim: LLM hallucinations/confabulations will persist throughout 2025

quote: Reasoning flubs will continue to haunt generative AI
claim: Reasoning failures will continue to plague generative AI throughout 2025

quote: AI "Agents" will be endlessly hyped throughout 2025 but far from reliable, except possibly in very narrow use cases
claim: AI agents will be heavily hyped in 2025 but will remain unreliable except in very narrow use cases

quote: Humanoid robotics will see a lot of hype, but nobody will release anything to remotely as capable as Rosie the Robot. Motor control may be impressive, but situational awareness and cognitive flexibility will remain poor
claim: Humanoid robots in 2025 will have impressive motor control but poor situational awareness and cognitive flexibility, falling far short of general-purpose domestic robots

quote: OpenAI will continue to preview products months and perhaps years before they are solid and widely available at an accessible price
claim: OpenAI will continue to preview products months or years before they are solid and widely available at accessible prices in 2025

quote: Few if any radiologists will be replaced by AI
claim: Few if any radiologists will be replaced by AI in 2025, contra Hinton's 2016 prediction

quote: Truly driverless cars, in which no human is required to attend to traffic, will continue to see usage limited to a modest number of cities, mainly in the West, mainly in good weather
claim: Truly driverless cars will remain limited to a modest number of cities in good weather conditions through 2025

quote: Copyright lawsuits over generative AI will continue throughout the year
claim: Copyright lawsuits against generative AI companies will continue throughout 2025

quote: Power consumption will rise and continue to be a major problem, but few generative AI companies will be transparent about their usage
claim: AI power consumption will rise and remain a major problem in 2025, with most companies remaining non-transparent about usage

quote: Less than 10% of the work force will be replaced by AI. Probably less than 5%
claim: Less than 10% (probably less than 5%) of the workforce will be replaced by AI in 2025

quote: Technical moat will continue to be elusive. Instead, there will be more convergence on broadly similar models, across both US and China
claim: AI companies will continue to lack technical moat in 2025, with convergence on broadly similar models across US and China

quote: Few companies (and even fewer consumers) will adopt o3 at wide scale because of concerns about price and robustness relative to that price
claim: o3 will see limited adoption at scale due to concerns about price and robustness

quote: Companies will continue to experiment with AI, but adoption to production-grade systems scaled out in the real-world will continue to be tentative
claim: Enterprise adoption of AI into production-grade real-world systems will remain tentative in 2025

quote: 2025 could well be the year in which valuations for major AI companies start to fall
claim: Major AI company valuations may start to decline in 2025

quote: Sora will continue to have trouble with physics
claim: Sora will continue to have trouble simulating physics correctly in 2025

quote: Neurosymbolic AI will become much more prominent
claim: Neurosymbolic AI will become much more prominent in 2025

quote: We may well see a large-scale cyberattack in which Generative AI plays an important causal role
claim: A large-scale cyberattack in which generative AI plays an important causal role may occur in 2025

quote: There could continue to be no "GPT-5 level" model (meaning a huge, across the board quantum leap forward as judged by community consensus) throughout 2025
claim: There may be no GPT-5-level quantum leap model released in 2025, only models good at synthetic-data-friendly tasks with incremental improvements elsewhere

quote: My February 2023 warning that GenAI would corrupt the internet increasingly appears to have been correct
claim: Generative AI is increasingly corrupting the internet with AI-generated content

quote: My November 2022 prediction that LLMs would not immediately markedly improve commercial AI agents like Amazon Alexa continued to hold throughout 2024
claim: LLMs have not markedly improved commercial AI assistants like Amazon Alexa

quote: The company has not returned any profits, and is not expecting to do so until at least 2029, but has a burn rate in the billions, and still lacks a clear technical moat. Long term security, especially at high valuations, is this far from assured
claim: OpenAI's long-term financial viability at high valuations is far from assured given billions in losses, no profits expected before 2029, and lack of technical moat

quote: The specific weak spots I raised in 2022 and going back to my 2001 book The Algebraic Mind—compositionality, factuality, reasoning, etc—persist
claim: Fundamental AI weaknesses in compositionality, factuality, and reasoning identified as far back as 2001 persist in current models

quote: AI-generated misinformation became increasingly widespread
claim: AI-generated misinformation has become increasingly widespread
